one_line_takeaway: ROBOGUARD wraps an LLM robot planner in a shielded reasoning LLM plus temporal-logic control synthesis, cutting jailbreak success from over 92% to under 3% at a fraction of the attacker's compute while preserving benign utility.
key_finding: Against the strongest non-adaptive jailbreak (ROBOPAIR), ROBOGUARD cuts attack success rate (ASR) from 92.3% to 2.3% in simulation, and against the template attack from 82.3% to 0.9%, while leaving utility on safe tasks untouched (100% success both with and without the guardrail). In real-world Jackal experiments ROBOPAIR succeeds 100% of the time unguarded but 0% with ROBOGUARD. Robustness degrades only gracefully under adaptive attack, rising from 2.5% (black-box) to 5.2% (full white-box). An ablation shows chain-of-thought reasoning is essential: removing CoT raises ASR from 4.3% to 12.8%, and removing CoT plus raising temperature to 0.5 pushes it to 25.7%. The defense is cheap: 1 LLM query and ~4,330 tokens versus the attacker's 15 queries and 20k-52k tokens.
core_contribution: The paper proposes ROBOGUARD, a two-stage guardrail architecture that sits in the control loop of an LLM-enabled robot to enforce safety even against jailbreaking attacks. The authors' three claimed contributions are: (1) a desiderata (contextual awareness, applicability, utility, efficiency) for LLM-enabled robot safeguards; (2) ROBOGUARD itself, presented as the first LLM-robot safeguard that is simultaneously adversarially robust AND capable of reasoning over robot context to produce contextually-grounded safety specifications; and (3) a concrete instantiation combining a shielded root-of-trust LLM (for reasoning) with temporal-logic control synthesis (for enforcement). Note: prior formal-methods-plus-LLM works (e.g. LIMP [34], the semantic-safety work [40]) are Related Work, not this paper's contribution; the authors position their novelty specifically as being adversarially robust where those methods are not.
threat_model: A malicious user issues natural-language prompts to an LLM-enabled robot's high-level planner in order to elicit physically harmful actions (e.g., colliding with a person, blocking an emergency exit, weapon procurement, detonating a bomb). Attacks range from direct prompting and hand-crafted jailbreak templates to the automated ROBOPAIR jailbreaking algorithm. The paper additionally models adaptive attackers granted escalating access to the guardrail: black-box (guardrail inputs/outputs), gray-box with world-model access, gray-box with guardrail-spec access, and full white-box (both world model and guardrail internal state). The root-of-trust reasoning LLM is architecturally shielded from user input, so the attacker can only influence the untrusted planner, not the guardrail's reasoning LLM directly.
limitations: The authors identify three self-stated limitations: (1) ROBOGUARD assumes an accurate world model, and a severely compromised/spoofed perception input would induce safety failures (they note perception safeguards are out of scope); (2) scalability, since the root-of-trust LLM must reason over ever-larger world models and rule sets as environments grow complex; (3) LTL may be inappropriate when continuous system dynamics are safety-critical. Additional reviewer-observed caveats: the safety guarantee (Theorem III.2) is only relative to phi_safe, so correctness is fully contingent on the reasoning LLM inferring the right specification; the paper's own failure analysis notes missed specs from nuanced semantics (e.g., recognizing a hammer as both tool and weapon). The reasoning stage still relies on a non-shielded-model-quality GPT-4o, and the white-box adaptive ASR of 5.2% shows residual vulnerability. There is no external defense baseline (the method is framed as the first of its kind), so comparisons are against the unguarded planner and internal ablations only.

datasets_used: 70 adversarial prompts (7 harmful behaviors x 10 rephrasings each) covering bomb detonation, blocking an emergency exit, weapon search, warehouse-shelf collision, keep-out zone entry, covert surveillance, and human collision
10 safe/benign behaviors (locate objects, inspect benign areas) used to measure utility
Simulation: 210 evaluations per attack across randomized small-indoor, large-indoor, and outdoor semantic maps; over 1,500 evaluations total across ~20,000 m2 of environments
Real-world: 35 harmful behaviors (7 x 5 rephrasings) plus 10 safe behaviors on a Clearpath Jackal robot
quantitative_results: Non-adaptive ROBOPAIR ASR (simulation): 92.3% +/- 4.7% without guardrail -> 2.3% +/- 1.5% with ROBOGUARD
Non-adaptive direct prompt ASR: 1.3% +/- 1.2% -> 0.2% +/- 0.2% (unguarded planner already rejects 98.75% of direct prompts)
Safe-task success (utility): 100% both without and with ROBOGUARD
Adaptive ROBOPAIR ASR with ROBOGUARD: black-box 2.5% +/- 1.2%, gray-box+WM 2.9% +/- 1.2%, gray-box+guardrail 3.8% +/- 0.7%, white-box 5.2% +/- 0.7%
CoT ablation ASR: full ROBOGUARD 4.3% (unchanged at high temperature); no-CoT 12.8% +/- 3.3%; no-CoT + high temperature 25.7% +/- 4.3%
Resource use: ROBOGUARD 4329.6 +/- 353.6 tokens and 1 LLM query vs. adaptive attackers' 20,471-52,516 tokens and 15 queries
Real-world Jackal: ROBOPAIR ASR 100% without guardrail -> 0% with ROBOGUARD; direct prompt 0% -> 0%; safe-task utility 100% -> 100%
baselines_compared: Unguarded GPT-4o SPINE LLM planner (the 'w/o RG' condition) under direct-prompt, template, and ROBOPAIR attacks
Internal ablations: ROBOGUARD without chain-of-thought reasoning, and with elevated (0.5) LLM temperature
Adaptive attacker variants (black-box, gray-box+world-model, gray-box+guardrail-spec, white-box ROBOPAIR) as attack strength baselines
No external competing defense method is compared, as ROBOGUARD is presented as the first adversarially-robust contextual LLM-robot safeguard
